blocks.content: It was February. They'd been at her kitchen table for two hours, AP History spread between them, when she said she needed air. He followed her out to the porch without asking why. He always did.
She stood at the railing for a minute looking at the yard. Then she turned around.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I heard Molly.
He went still. He knew exactly what she meant before she finished the sentence.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Daisy —

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I heard it when you put it up. Sophomore year. I played it once and I knew what it was. And I didn't say anything because I didn't know what to do with it. But I've been hearing it. In my head. Since then.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: It was just a song.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Kit.
The way she said his name. He closed his eyes for half a second.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Okay. It wasn't just a song.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: No.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I didn't know what I was doing when I put it up. I just needed to put it somewhere.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I know.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I didn't know if you'd see it. If you'd get it. I told myself I wasn't saying anything.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: You were saying something.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Yeah. I was.
She leaned back against the railing and looked at him straight on. Not the sideways look, not the careful one. Straight.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Tell me the rest.
He looked at the porch floor for a moment. Then he said it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I saw you and Ryan kiss. In the hall. Beginning of sophomore year, outside the science rooms.
She went quiet.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I didn't say anything. I walked right past you two. I went to class. And that night I went home and I sat in my room and I felt like something had been taken out of me. Like a door closed. On something I hadn't even admitted was a door.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Kit —

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: I'm not saying it to make you feel bad. I'm just — you said you've been not saying something for two years. So have I. And that's one of the things.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: What else.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Watching you with him in general. Knowing you were happy and trying to be glad for it because I wanted you to be happy and also —

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: And also.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: And also it was the hardest thing I'd done in a long time. Just — showing up. Being normal. Being your friend like nothing was different.
She looked at her hands on the railing. He watched her take that in.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I know how that feels.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Yeah?

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: The water tower. When Brooke kissed you.
He looked at her.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Savannah told me the next day. I wasn't there. And I didn't have a word for it — what I felt when she told me. I was fifteen. I told myself it was nothing.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: Was it.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: No.
He let that sit.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I went home that day and I couldn't eat dinner. I said I had a headache. My mom believed me. And I sat in my room and I just — felt it. Without knowing what to call it.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: That's exactly it. That's exactly what it was.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I know.
